summary: This lesson teaches that nouns ending in -ma are masculine (from Greek neuter), introduces the verb sentir (to feel), and practices the reflexive 'lost myself' pattern with me/te/nos to mean 'got lost'. Extensive practice combining haber with reflexive pronouns and emphatic subject pronouns.

teachings.summary: A small group of nouns ending in -ma are masculine, not feminine. These words come from Greek, where they were neuter (which looks like masculine). When they entered Spanish via Latin, they were treated as masculine.
teachings.rule: Nouns ending in -ma are typically masculine (Greek origin): el problema, el sistema, el programa, el aroma, el panorama, el clima.
teachings.notes: A very common error is saying 'la problema'. These words come from Greek (problema=provlima, system=systima). Greek neuter words look like masculine and were adopted as masculine in Spanish.

teachings.summary: Sentir means to feel, related to 'sensation'. Can also mean to perceive/smell. Past participle is sentido.

teachings.conceptName: Reflexive verbs: perderse = to get lost
teachings.summary: To say 'I got lost' in Spanish, you say 'I have lost myself' (me he perdido). The reflexive pronouns me/te/nos go before haber. Combined with emphatic subject pronouns: yo me he perdido, tú te has perdido, nosotros nos hemos perdido.
teachings.rule: Reflexive pronoun + haber + past participle. The reflexive pronoun goes before haber.

examples.english: I have got lost (I have lost myself)

teachings.notes: When me he sounds together, the two /e/ sounds blend. Just as 'me preparo' = I get ready (I ready myself), 'me he perdido' = I got lost (I have lost myself).

teachings.conceptName: Haber: auxiliary 'to have' for past tense
teachings.summary: Quick review of haber forms using the two-vowel mnemonic: e → he, hemos; a → ha, has, han.
